State of California

Memorandum

To : Bob S. Cina Nan Date : 3/22/76
Barbara John Rick
Mike Ken Ken G.
Dan Mark Ted
Del Mickey

From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
Natalie West

Subject: 86108(b) Filings

Pursuant to our conversation of Friday,
March 19, a client who pays $250 or more in any one
month to influence legislative or administrative action
must file pursuant to Section 86108(b).

The law firm or other entity retained by
the client is not subject to the reporting requirements.


NEW:pvp

Mickey, Dan, Ken, Natalie, Bob,                          March 16, 1976
Ken G., Nan


GINA
86018(b) Filings


Peter Brown, an attorney with the San Francisco law firm
of Orrick, Herrington, Rowley and Sutcliff, has requested
information regarding the filing obligation of a law firm
which receives money from a client to influence administrative action.

The question he raises is outlined in the February memos
attached but has never been resolved.

"The retained entity is subject to the filing
requirements if its payments to its employees
or others for the purpose of influencing
legislative or administrative action reach $250
per month in any month."

I told him someone would get back to him in a day or two
with an answer. Advice, please.


                                    The law firm is met
                                    are §6 10f(b) [illegible] - decided
                                    at op. reg. meeting of
                                    3/19/76

State of California

Memorandum

To     : Mickey Bennett
        Bob Stern
        Nan Hambleton

Date   : February 18, 1976

From   : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
        Dan Lowenstein

Subject:


        Peter Baumbusch testified in Los Angeles regarding
the issue raised in Nan's memorandum of February 5.  He
argues that the proposed Form 650 manual is incorrect
in stating that payments to employees to influence
legislative or administrative action are counted for
purposes of Section 86108(b).

        Baumbusch's position seems quite reasonable to me.
On the other hand, Nan's memo indicates that Rudy and
Bob disagree.  Let's discuss.

The image appears to be almost entirely blank/white with only a few very faint marks or artifacts visible at the top of the page. There is no clearly legible text to transcribe from this document image.

State of California

Memorandum

To : Mickey, Dan and Bob                                Date : February 5, 1976


From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
       Nan

Subject: 86108(b) Filings


       Peter Baumbusch called today with comments about the proposed
"Instruction Manual for Form 650 for Persons Spending $250 or
More to Influence Legislative or Administrative Action and for
Employers of Lobbyists." (The manual is now at the Printing
Plant; we haven't yet received the first proof.)

       The following statement appears on Page Two under the section
entitled "86108(b) Filers," a copy of which is attached.

       The retained entity is subject to the filing require-
       ments if its payments to its employees or to others
       for the purpose of influencing legislative or admin-
       istrative action reach $250 per month in any month.

       Baumbusch believes the above states a policy formulated by
the staff without consideration by the Commission.

       He believes the result of such a position would mean that
virtually every law firm in California who spends any time
lobbying on behalf of its clients (because very little legal
counsel may be obtained for less than $250), as well as many
public relations firms, advertising firms and many others
would be required to file statements (reporting exchanges.)
He said that the purpose of the lobbying provisions is to
reach clients to make sure they aren't making "deals" with
officials, and that ordinary citizens who aren't lobbyists
and who don't employ lobbyists shouldn't have to bear the
reporting burden. He thinks the discrimination is particu-
larly unjust between the individual lawyer versus a law firm.
If a client retains an individual lawyer, there is going to
be no reporting because the lawyer hasn't paid a salary to
anybody; whereas in a two-man firm, the firm is going to have
to start reporting in relation to things that don't make
sense. It's not the firm that wants something accomplished--
it's the client!

       He wants to raise the issue before the Commission and said
that he will be very miffed if the FPPC publishes the manual
without giving him a chance to state his case. He doesn't

I can see this image appears to be mostly blank or very faintly printed, with only some small marks or symbols visible at the top (appearing to be decorative elements or page indicators). The rest of the page is white/empty with no clearly legible text to transcribe.

Memo to Mickey, Dan and [illegible]
Page 2
February 5, 1976


believe the Commission has heard his side. He said that he
doubts many people have read the proposed manual, and that
if it is published as drafted, there will be many, many
unhappy people in a few months after they realize the impact
of the instructions.

He has argued this point before with Rudy and Bob, both of
whom disagree with him.

He knows that the Commission will be holding hearings in
Los Angeles soon, and he knows the purpose of them; however,
if appropriate, he would like to air this matter before the
Commission then or at any other suitable time.

HE WOULD LIKE FOR SOMEONE TO CALL HIM TO MAKE ARRANGEMENTS.

The image appears to be almost entirely blank or white, with only some faint marks or artifacts visible at the top (appearing as small curved shapes). There is no clearly legible text content to transcribe from this document image.

Instruction Manual                                    Page Two

     the individual qualifies as a lobbyist because of the work done
on the present engagement, then the person having hired the firm
becomes an employer of a lobbyist, and the last two of public
relations firm which contain the lobbyist (i.e., the direct
employer) becomes an employer of lobbyists and subject to the
filing requirements. (See discussion on Page ____)

     If a person retains another entity for the purpose of
influencing or attempting to influence legislative or administrative action and the individual assigned to the job does not
qualify as a lobbyist, then both retaining person retaining the law
or public relations firm retained firm itself become
subject to the filing requirements of §86108(b) (see below) for
every month in which payments of either of the parties reach
$250.

     86108(b) Filers: All persons not employers of lobbyists
who spend $250 or more in any one month in influencing or
attempting to influence legislative or administrative action
must file periodic reports.

     Prospective filers should note that not all actions by a
state agency are included. A definition of "influencing" and
of "administrative action" is found on Page ___ of these
instructions. It should also be noted that not all payments
are includible in determining whether a prospective 86108(b)
filer has reached the $250 threshold. The discussion of
includible (and excludible) payments starting on Page ____ 
of these instructions should be followed.

     Persons who make such payments to a business entity
retained to influence legislative or administrative action on
their behalf who do not qualify as employers of lobbyists
because the individual in the retained firm doing the work is
not a lobbyist are subject to the filing requirements for each
month in which their payments to the retained entity reach
$250. The retained entity is subject to filing requirements
if its payments to its employees or to others for the purpose
of influencing legislative or administrative action reach $250
per month in any month.

     Prospective 86103(b) filers should also note that if the
only payments they made were those which directly benefited
elective, legislative, or agency officials, or members of their
families, then no reporting obligation is created for the
person making such payments. (Definitions of these officials
are found on Page ____ of these instructions.) If any payments
other than those which benefit such officials are made during
the month, then the payments which directly benefit officials
and the other payments must be combined to determine whether a
filing obligation under 86103(b) exists.

The image appears to be almost entirely blank or white, with only some very faint marks or artifacts visible at the top (possibly loading indicators or page artifacts). There is no clearly legible text content to transcribe from this document image.

Instruction Manual                                    Page Three


     The data required to be reported is generally the same
regardless of whether the filing is required by virtue of
§6108(a) or §6108(b). However, the timing of the filings may
differ. See "When to File" on Page ___ of these instructions.


                    III. Hiring a Lobbyist

     If an individual is engaged to lobby and qualifies or knows
that he or she will qualify as a lobbyist, the employer must
authorize that person to act on the employer's behalf. A Form
61A, "Lobbyist Activity Authorization," must be executed and is
required to be filed by the lobbyist at the same time as he or
she files the required registration form. The hired lobbyist
cannot perform any of the functions of a lobbyist until the
Authorization and Registration Statements are properly on file
with the Secretary of State. (§§86100-86101)

     So long as the individual retained does not qualify and
does not anticipate qualifying as a lobbyist, no authorization
need be filed. However, once the individual is required to
register, each of the clients for whom he or she lobbies must
file an authorization (Form 61A) for that lobbyist.

     It is important to note that a new authorization must be
filed within twenty days after the start of any regular bi-annual
session of the Legislature. This new authorization must be
filed by all lobbyists, even those who do not lobby the Legisla-
ture.


     IV. Hiring a Law Firm, Lobby Firm, or Other Business Entity
         to Influence Legislative or Administrative Action

     Frequently, a business entity such as a governmental relations
firm, a public relations firm, or a law firm may be engaged by
another person to influence legislative and/or administrative
action.

     If the individual or individuals assigned by the retained
entity to do the work or be responsible for the work are lobbyists
because they have previously qualified as lobbyists, or if they
become lobbyists because of the present assignment, or anticipate
qualifying as lobbyists by reason of the present assignment, then
the person retaining the firm itself and the retained firm itself
are both employers of lobbyists, and each must file an Authorization
(Form 61A) and file required periodic reports (Form 650). (2 Cal.
Forms 61A and the required periodic reports. Their differing responsibilities are as follows:
Adm. Code §16619)
